Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after soccer practice or after hanging out with friends, but it rarely happened the way I planned. Most of my studying happened late at night when I finally felt guilty enough to open my laptop. I'd skim through readings and highlight stuff, but I wouldn't say it was organized at all.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then cram when a deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd have a good week where I kept up, but then something would come up with my extracurriculars or I'd just feel bored and unmotivated, and the whole routine would fall apart again.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due or I got an email reminder. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was forgetting about. It wasn't really a habit—more like damage control when I realized I was falling behind.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I logged in more regularly, but it never stuck. The platform just felt like another thing on my to-do list that I kept putting off. I never got to the point where checking it felt automatic or natural.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful because I could download them and read them on my own time. I'm a read-write kind of learner, so having text I could highlight and take notes on worked better for me than videos. The discussion posts were okay too because they forced me to think about the material, even if I didn't always put much effort into them.
Q6: I barely watched the videos—they felt way too long and I'd zone out halfway through. I'd open them sometimes and let them play in the background while doing other stuff, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. The optional readings and extra resources I pretty much ignored completely because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd actually do it. Activities that felt abstract or disconnected from my grade just didn't seem worth the effort. I also needed things to be relatively quick—if something looked like it would take forever, I'd put it off indefinitely.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, I couldn't motivate myself to do it. Required activities at least had consequences, so I'd force myself through them even when I was bored. The optional ones just felt like extra work I didn't have energy for.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone and read them during bus rides or when I was waiting around. I also used my own notes a lot—I'd write things out by hand because it helps me remember better. Sometimes I'd look stuff up on YouTube or Google if the course materials weren't making sense.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour or two going through my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend from class sometimes, quizzing each other or talking through concepts, and obviously that's invisible to the system.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a reading, open it once in the LMS, and then never go back to the platform for that material. I'd have it saved on my laptop or phone and reference it multiple times while studying, but the system would only show that one initial access.
Q12: Around midterms things got really hard. I had a lot going on with my soccer team and I was feeling pretty anxious about everything piling up. Even though I wasn't super stressed in general, the anxiety would hit me in waves and make it hard to focus. I'd just feel bored and disconnected from the material.
Q13: For sure. When I had games or practice, schoolwork always took a backseat. I'd come home exhausted and the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS. Confusion was a big factor too—when I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help. It just felt easier to ignore it.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through stuff but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd read the same paragraph three times without absorbing anything. It looked like I was engaged but I was really just going through the motions.
Q15: All the time. I knew the exam prep materials were important but I kept putting them off because they seemed overwhelming. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd find something else to do. I think the anxiety made me avoid things that felt too big to tackle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do stuff. I'd have this burst of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. Without a deadline, I'd just drift and let things slide. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I functioned.
Q17: Honestly, I didn't pay much attention to feedback. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If I got a bad grade I'd feel discouraged for a bit, but it didn't really change how I approached the next assignment. I know that's probably why my exam score was so low.
Q18: The structure was okay but it didn't really push me to stay engaged. Having five online courses at once meant everything blurred together. If the course had more built-in checkpoints or required participation, I might have stayed on track better. But it was easy to fall behind without anyone noticing.
Q19: The flexibility was supposed to help but it actually made things harder for me. Without set class times, I'd just keep postponing everything. The readings being available to download was good, but the video content being so long made it easy to skip. More interactive stuff might have kept me engaged.
Q20: I think some people might be like me—clicking around, opening things, but not really absorbing anything. You can spend a lot of time in the LMS without actually learning if you're distracted or unmotivated. High activity doesn't mean high quality engagement, that's for sure.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me. If someone's a good reader and studies offline a lot, their LMS activity would be low but they could still do well. I tried to do that but my exam performance shows I wasn't actually retaining enough. Some people are just better at efficient studying than others.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the anxiety that keeps you from focusing. It's just a surface-level picture.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real mental effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being curious and wanting to understand. I wasn't really engaged this semester, even when I was technically doing the work.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling internally with anxiety and boredom. It wouldn't show the mindfulness exercises I tried to do to cope, or the times I genuinely wanted to engage but just couldn't make myself focus. It also wouldn't show that I actually did care about passing—I just couldn't translate that into consistent effort.
